name: Asthma attack
summary: Airways narrow — hard to breathe. Most children with asthma carry a blue (ventolin/salbutamol) inhaler.
recognition: Hard to speak in full sentences
Severe: blue-grey tinge to lips and nails

callEmergency.text: Call 999 if they're getting worse despite using their inhaler, can't speak, or lose consciousness.

steps: Encourage them to use their blue inhaler. Help if needed. Effect should be felt within minutes.
Sit them UPRIGHT in a comfortable position. Do NOT lie them down.
Keep them upright even if they get weaker.
Only lay them down if they become unconscious.
Stay with them, keep them upright.
If they become unconscious or stop breathing normally — start CPR.

warnings.text: Don't take an asthmatic outside for "fresh air" — cold air makes attacks worse.
Don't lay them down (unless unconscious) — sitting upright helps them breathe.
